comment_text.snp4.2:2.2: Te duppamuñca has been taken by most translators as referring to people, in senses either active (Bodhi’s “let go with difficulty”) or passive (Norman’s “are hard to release”). However the phrase occurs in a very similar context at SN 3.10:5.2 and Dhp 346:2, where it refers to the chains that are hard to escape.
comment_text.snp4.2:2.4: Jappa means both to “incant” and to “long for”, and the English “pray’ has exactly the same connotations—for exactly the same reasons. They originated in begging favors of a god.
comment_text.snp4.2:5.3: “Jaws of death” (maccumukha) is also at SN 2.22:7.3. Compare Kaṭha Upaniṣad 2.1.1, “he escapes from the jaws of death” (tanmṛtyumukhātpramucyate).
comment_text.snp4.2:7.1: The reference here is to the two ends of contact (AN 6.61) rather than the two extremes of views or paths.
comment_text.snp4.2:8.1: Bodhi, Norman, and Ñāṇadīpa, apparently following the commentary, take vitareyya as an optative. But the previous verse spoke of an arahant, and here the one who has fully understood perception must have already crossed over. Vitareyya oghaṁ occurs at Snp 3.5:13.2, where vitareyya is clearly an absolutive, and is rendered as such by both Bodhi and Norman.

root_text.snp4.2:1.4: Kāmā hi loke na hi suppahāyā.
root_text.snp4.2:2.4: Ime va kāme purime va jappaṁ.

translation_text.snp4.2:1.1: Trapped in a cave, thickly overspread,
translation_text.snp4.2:1.3: That sort is far from seclusion,
translation_text.snp4.2:1.4: for sensual pleasures in the world <j>are not easy to give up.
translation_text.snp4.2:2.1: The chains of desire, the bonds of life’s pleasures
translation_text.snp4.2:2.2: are hard to escape, for one cannot free another.
translation_text.snp4.2:2.3: Looking to the past or the future,
translation_text.snp4.2:2.4: they pray for these pleasures or former ones.
translation_text.snp4.2:3.1: Greedy, fixated, infatuated by sensual pleasures,
translation_text.snp4.2:3.3: When led to suffering they lament,
translation_text.snp4.2:3.4: “What will become of us <j>when we pass away from here?”
translation_text.snp4.2:4.1: That’s why a personage should train in this life:
translation_text.snp4.2:4.2: should you know that anything in the world is wrong,
translation_text.snp4.2:4.3: don’t act wrongly on account of that;
translation_text.snp4.2:4.4: for the attentive say this life is short.
translation_text.snp4.2:5.1: I see the world’s population floundering,
translation_text.snp4.2:5.2: given to craving for future lives.
translation_text.snp4.2:5.3: Base men wail in the jaws of death,
translation_text.snp4.2:5.4: not rid of craving for life after life.
translation_text.snp4.2:6.2: like fish in puddles of a dried-up stream.
translation_text.snp4.2:6.4: forming no attachment to future lives.
translation_text.snp4.2:7.1: Rid of desire for both ends,
translation_text.snp4.2:7.2: having completely understood contact, free of greed,
translation_text.snp4.2:7.3: doing nothing for which they’d blame themselves,
translation_text.snp4.2:7.4: the attentive don’t cling to the seen and the heard.
translation_text.snp4.2:8.1: Having completely understood perception <j>and having crossed the flood,
translation_text.snp4.2:8.2: the sage, not clinging to possessions,
translation_text.snp4.2:8.3: with dart plucked out, living diligently,
translation_text.snp4.2:8.4: does not hope for this world or the next.
